differences.title: 1. INITIAL LOAD
differences.leftDesc: Reduces client-side JavaScript, leading to faster initial page loads.
differences.rightDesc: Requires more JavaScript to be downloaded and parsed by the browser.

differences.title: 2. INTERACTIVITY
differences.leftDesc: Can't use state or effects directly; needs hydration for interactivity.
differences.rightDesc: Can use state, effects, and event listeners natively in the browser.

differences.title: 3. DATA FETCHING
differences.leftDesc: Can directly access databases and backend resources without extra layers.
differences.rightDesc: Must fetch data via API endpoints, adding network latency.

differences.title: 4. SEO
differences.leftDesc: Content is rendered server-side, making it easily discoverable by search engines.
differences.rightDesc: Relies on client-side rendering; search engines might need to execute JS.

differences.title: 5. SECURITY
differences.leftDesc: Sensitive logic and data access remain on the server, reducing exposure.
differences.rightDesc: Client-side code can be inspected; API security is crucial.

differences.title: 6. CODE SHARING
differences.leftDesc: Enables sharing code between server and client environments seamlessly.
differences.rightDesc: Code primarily runs in the browser, limiting server-side execution.

differences.title: 7. DEVELOPMENT
differences.leftDesc: Requires understanding of server rendering and hydration concepts.
differences.rightDesc: Leverages existing client-side JavaScript knowledge and patterns.
